text: <par>Galo seems to show an alternation between <latinform>bu</latinform> and <latinform>pa</latinform>. If these are indeed allofams, it would support the reconstruction of a rising diphthong <latinform>*-wa-</latinform>.</par>
<par>Check gloss’ s spelling; should it be “beneficent” ?</par>
<par>Check gloss’ s spelling; should it be “beneficent” ?</par>

<par><latinform>lii</latinform> appears in the words for various breeding animals in Galo:  <latinform>ho◦lii</latinform> 'breeding mithun', <latinform>rog◦lii</latinform> 'breeding fowl', <latinform>rəg◦lii</latinform> 'breeding pig', <latinform>bin◦lii</latinform> 'breeding goat', <latinform>jab◦lii</latinform> 'breeding duck', and <latinform>ə◦lii</latinform> 'breeding cow'.  It also appears in <latinform>a◦lii</latinform>, which has a number of apparently related glosses: "heritage", "clan", "seed (heirloom)", "seed", "lineage", and "breeder". lii also appears in the compounds <latinform>lii◦luk</latinform> "reborn", <latinform>lii◦nam</latinform> "fruit (v.i.)", <latinform>lii◦nam</latinform> "propagate".  <latinform>-lii</latinform> is glossed as "new (adj.)"; this may be related to the morpheme that appears in the words for breeding animals.  <latinform>lii</latinform> also appears in various words for easily spread diseases:  <latinform>ci◦lii-ram◦lii</latinform> "epidemic", "virus", <latinform>hi◦lii</latinform> "plague",<latinform>buu◦lii</latinform> "plague (of rats)", <latinform>rɨ◦lii</latinform> "plague", "communicable disease", "epidemic" ; this may be related to the "propagate" sense of <latinform>lii</latinform>. [Liberty Lidz]</par>
